---
title: "book FFR Eng"
author: "謝慕揚 MD, PhD, FESC"
date: 2026-03-27
lang: zh-TW
toc: true
toc-depth: 3
number-sections: true
---

\maketitle
\tableofcontents

---


## Theoretical Foundation


### FFR Definition and Principles


Fractional Flow Reserve (FFR) is a physiological index used to assess the hemodynamic significance of coronary artery stenosis.


#### Basic Definition


equation
FFR = P_d{P_a}
equation

Where:

- $P_d$ = Distal coronary pressure (pressure distal to stenosis)
- $P_a$ = Aortic pressure (proximal pressure)


#### Theoretical Derivation


FFR represents the ratio of maximum achievable blood flow in the presence of stenosis to the theoretical maximum flow without stenosis in the myocardial territory supplied by that coronary artery.

According to Poiseuille's law, blood flow is proportional to the pressure gradient:

equation
Q = \Delta P{R}
equation

Under maximal hyperemia (induced by adenosine):

equation
FFR = Q_{max,stenosis}{Q_{max,normal}} = (P_d - P_v)/R_{myo}{(P_a - P_v)/R_{myo}} \approx P_d{P_a}
equation

When $P_v$ (central venous pressure) is much smaller than $P_a$ and $P_d$, this simplifies to $FFR = P_d/P_a$.


#### Clinical Significance


| L{11cm}} Clinical Interpretation of FFR Values |  |
| --- | --- |
| **FFR Value** | **Clinical Significance** |
| $FFR > 0.80$ | No hemodynamic significance, PCI not recommended |
| $FFR \leq 0.80$ | Hemodynamically significant, PCI recommended |
| $FFR < 0.75$ | Definite ischemia, strong recommendation for intervention |
| $0.75 < FFR < 0.80$ | Gray zone, requires clinical judgment |
| $FFR < 0.50$ | Severe ischemia, high-risk lesion |


## Technical Aspects


### Measurement Technique


#### Equipment Requirements


- Pressure wire
- Hyperemic agents: Adenosine (140-180 $\mu$g/kg/min IV) or IC bolus (40-80 $\mu$g)
- Pressure measurement system
- Angiography equipment


#### Measurement Protocol


| L{12cm}} Standard FFR Measurement Procedure |  |
| --- | --- |
| **Step** | **Operational Details** |
| 1 | **Calibration:** Zero the pressure wire and measurement system |
| 2 | **Equalization:** Confirm $P_d = P_a$ at guide catheter position |
| 3 | **Wire advancement:** Advance pressure wire distal to lesion |
| 4 | **Induce hyperemia:** Administer adenosine for maximal hyperemia |
| 5 | **Record pressure:** Record $P_d$ and $P_a$ during hyperemia |
| 6 | **Calculate FFR:** Automatic or manual calculation of $FFR = P_d/P_a$ |
| 7 | **Pullback verification:** Withdraw wire to guide catheter to check for drift |


### Non-Hyperemic Indices


#### iFR (Instantaneous Wave-free Ratio)


iFR is measured during a specific period of the cardiac cycle (wave-free period) without hyperemic agents:


- Measurement timing: Mid-diastole (approximately 25
- Cutoff: $iFR \leq 0.89$ considered significant
- Advantages: No adenosine required, shorter procedure time, fewer side effects
- Correlation with FFR: Approximately 80-85


## Wheatstone Bridge Principle


### Application to Pressure Measurement


Pressure wire systems utilize the Wheatstone Bridge principle to convert mechanical pressure into electrical signals.


#### Basic Bridge Structure


The bridge consists of four resistors, with at least one being a pressure-sensitive resistor (piezoresistive sensor):

equation
V_{out} = V_{in} \left(R_3{R_3 + R_4} - R_2{R_1 + R_2}\right)
equation

When pressure changes, the resistance of the pressure-sensitive element changes, causing a voltage output variation that allows pressure measurement.


#### Application in FFR Systems


| L{10cm}} Wheatstone Bridge Components in FFR Systems |  |
| --- | --- |
| **Component** | **Function** |
| Pressure sensor | Located at wire tip, contains piezoresistive elements |
| Signal conversion | Converts pressure changes to voltage changes |
| Amplifier | Amplifies minute voltage signals |
| Calibration system | Eliminates temperature, drift, and other interference |
| Digital conversion | Converts analog signals to digital pressure readings |


### Technical Considerations


- **Drift:** Prolonged measurements may cause signal drift; calibration before and after is essential
- **Temperature compensation:** Body temperature variations can affect resistance values
- **Sensitivity:** Modern pressure wires can detect pressure changes within ±2 mmHg


---


## FAME 1 & FAME 2 Trials


### FAME 1 (2009)


| L{10cm}} FAME 1 Trial Detailed Information |  |
| --- | --- |
| **Category** | **Details** |
| Full Name | Fractional Flow Reserve versus Angiography for Multivessel Evaluation |
| Publication Year | 2009 |
| Journal | [New England Journal of Medicine](https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJMoa0807611) |
| Study Design | Multicenter RCT |
| Sample Size | 1,005 patients |
| Inclusion Criteria | Multivessel coronary artery disease suitable for PCI |
| Exclusion Criteria | STEMI <5 days, history of CABG |
| Intervention | **FFR arm:** Treat only lesions with FFR $\leq 0.80$     **Angio arm:** Treat all lesions with diameter stenosis $\geq 50 |
| Primary Endpoint | 1-year MACE (death, MI, repeat revascularization) |
| Primary Results | FFR arm MACE: 13.2  HR: 0.72 (95 |
| Secondary Results | FFR arm used fewer stents (1.9 vs 2.7, P<0.001)     Similar procedure time     Similar contrast volume |
| 5-Year Follow-up | MACE: 28  FFR arm significantly better in first 2 years, similar from 2-5 years |


### FAME 2 (2012-2018)


| L{10cm}} FAME 2 Trial Comprehensive Analysis |  |
| --- | --- |
| **Category** | **Details** |
| Full Name | Fractional Flow Reserve-Guided PCI versus Medical Therapy in Stable Coronary Disease |
| Publication Year | 2012 (initial), 2018 (5-year) |
| Journal | [New England Journal of Medicine](https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJMoa1205361) |
| Study Design | Multicenter RCT |
| Sample Size | 1,220 patients with FFR $\leq 0.80$ |
| Inclusion Criteria | Stable CAD with at least one stenosis and FFR $\leq 0.80$ |
| Randomization | **PCI + OMT arm:** 888 patients     **OMT only arm:** 332 patients     **Registry (FFR >0.80):** 166 patients |
| Primary Endpoint (2-year) | Death, MI, or urgent revascularization     PCI + OMT: 4.3  HR: 0.32 (95 |
| Components | **Death:** 0.9  **MI:** 3.4  **Urgent revascularization:** 1.6 |
| 5-Year Results | **Primary endpoint:** 13.9  HR: 0.46 (95  **Death or MI:** 8.1  HR: 0.66 (95 |
| Registry Patients | FFR >0.80 with medical therapy only     5-year event rate: 9.0  Lower than both randomized arms |
| Key Message | FFR-guided PCI significantly reduces urgent revascularization     Trend toward reduced death/MI at 5 years     Validates FFR $\leq 0.80$ as intervention threshold |


---


## Complete Revascularization in ACS


### Landmark Trials in STEMI with Multivessel Disease


| L{2.5cm}L{2cm}L{6cm}} Major Complete Revascularization Trials in STEMI |  |
| --- | --- |
| **Trial** | **Journal/Year** |
| PRAMI | [NEJM 2013](https://www.nejm.org/doi/full/10.1056/NEJMoa1305520) |
| CvLPRIT | [JACC 2015](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0735109715000819?via |
| COMPLETE | [NEJM 2019](https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJMoa1907775) |
| MULTISTARS AMI | [NEJM 2023](https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJMoa2307823) |
| FULL REVASC | https://www.nejm.org/doi/10.1056/NEJMoa2314149?url_ver=Z39.88-2003 |
| FIRE | [NEJM 2023](https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJMoa2300468) |


### Timing of Complete Revascularization


| L{2.5cmL{2cm}L{6cm}} Immediate vs Staged Complete Revascularization |  |
| --- | --- |
| **Trial** | **Journal/Year** |
| COMPLETE Timing | [NEJM 2019](https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJMoa1907775) |
| MULTISTARS AMI | [NEJM 2023](https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJMoa2307823) |
| SMILE | [JACC 2016](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0735109715074902?via |


### Cardiogenic Shock Special Considerations


| L{2.5cm}L{2cm}L{6.5cm}} Revascularization Strategy in STEMI with Cardiogenic Shock |  |
| --- | --- |
| **Trial** | **Journal/Year** |
| CULPRIT-SHOCK | [NEJM 2017](https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJMoa1710261) |


### FFR-guided vs Angiography-guided Complete Revascularization


| L{2.5cm}L{2cm}L{6cm}} Comparison of FFR and Angiography-Guided Complete Revascularization |  |
| --- | --- |
| **Trial** | **Journal/Year** |
| DANAMI-3 PRIMULTI | [Lancet 2015](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0140673615606481?via |
| COMPARE-ACUTE | [NEJM 2017](https://www.nejm.org/doi/full/10.1056/NEJMoa1701067) |
| FLOWER-MI | [NEJM 2021](https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJMoa2104650) |
| FRAME-AMI | [European Heart J](https://academic.oup.com/eurheartj/article/44/6/473/6948120?login=true) |
| FULL REVASC | https://www.nejm.org/doi/10.1056/NEJMoa2314149?url_ver=Z39.88-2003 |


---


### Meta-Analyses and Systematic Reviews


| L{3cmL{7cm}} Important Meta-Analyses of Multivessel PCI in ACS |  |
| --- | --- |
| **Meta-analysis** | **Journal/Year** |
| Elgendy et al. | [JACC Cardiovasc Interv 2017](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1936879816321902) |
| Pavasini et al. | [Eur Heart J 2020](https://academic.oup.com/eurheartj/article/41/42/4103/5692630?login=true) |
| Biscaglia et al. | [PLOS ONE 2024](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12510650/) |
|  | JACC 2024 |


---


## Clinical Practice Guidelines Summary


### Current Guideline Recommendations


| L{10cm}} 2024 ACS Multivessel Disease Management Guidelines |  |
| --- | --- |
| **Clinical Scenario** | **Recommendations** |
| STEMI + MVD (no shock) | **Class I:** Complete revascularization recommended     Can be immediate or staged (in-hospital or post-discharge)     Consider FFR/iFR guidance for non-culprit lesions |
| STEMI + MVD + Shock | **Class I:** Culprit-only PCI     **Class IIa:** Consider staged revascularization     Based on CULPRIT-SHOCK trial |
| NSTEMI + MVD | **Class IIa:** Complete revascularization     Staged approach more common than immediate     Can use FFR/iFR to assess non-culprit lesions |
| Elderly Patients (≥75 years) | Based on FIRE trial     Physiology-guided complete revasc beneficial     Assess surgical risk and benefit |
| Presence of CTO | STEMI stable patients: Immediate CTO PCI not recommended     Cardiogenic shock: CTO should not be treated immediately     Consider subsequent staged intervention |


### FFR/iFR Usage Recommendations


| L{9cm}} Application of Physiologic Assessment in ACS |  |
| --- | --- |
| **Scenario** | **Recommendations and Evidence Level** |
| Stable CAD multivessel disease | **Class I, Level A**     FFR $\leq 0.80$ recommend PCI     FFR $>0.80$ can safely defer |
| STEMI non-culprit lesions | **Class IIa, Level B**     Can use FFR/iFR for assessment     Helps decide immediate or staged PCI |
| NSTEMI non-culprit lesions | **Class IIa, Level B**     FFR/iFR helps reduce unnecessary PCI     Safe and effective compared to angio-guided |
| Timing of measurement (ACS) | Index procedure or staged (days later) both acceptable     Studies show FFR/iFR values stable     iFR may be more suitable in acute phase (no adenosine needed) |
| Not recommended | Culprit lesion (clearly needs immediate PCI)     Cardiogenic shock (CULPRIT-SHOCK)     STEMI with left main disease |


---


## Controversies and Future Directions


### Current Controversies


| L{9.5cm}} Controversial Issues in FFR and Complete Revascularization |  |
| --- | --- |
| **Controversy** | **Discussion** |
| FLOWER-MI neutral results | FFR-guided failed to prove superiority over angio-guided     Possible reasons: Modern angio assessment already accurate, low event rate, trial design     Does not mean FFR is useless; both methods acceptable |
| Timing of complete revasc | Immediate vs staged still debated     MULTISTARS AMI showed non-inferiority     Immediate may increase contrast volume, procedural complexity     Staged requires rehospitalization, increases healthcare costs |
| FFR accuracy in ACS | Microvascular dysfunction may affect accuracy     Most studies show good reliability     iFR may be alternative option |
| FAME 2 soft endpoint issue | Main benefit from reduced urgent revascularization     No significant difference in death/MI (trend at 5 years)     Non-blinded design may introduce bias |
| Cost-effectiveness | FFR equipment cost vs reduced unnecessary PCI     Long-term follow-up shows cost-effective     Requires additional training and equipment |


### Future Research Directions


- **Integration of imaging and physiology:** Combined IVUS/OCT + FFR assessment
- **Novel physiologic indices:** dPR, RFR, virtual FFR (computed FFR)
- **Artificial intelligence applications:** AI-assisted FFR interpretation, outcome prediction
- **Special populations:** Optimal strategies for diabetes, chronic kidney disease, very elderly patients
- **Long-term outcomes:** >5-year follow-up of complete revascularization effects
- **Precision medicine:** Gene and biomarker-guided personalized treatment


---


## Key References


### Landmark Trials


1. 
2. Tonino PAL, De Bruyne B, Pijls NHJ, et al. Fractional flow reserve versus angiography for guiding percutaneous coronary intervention. [*N Engl J Med](https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJMoa0807611). 2009;360(3):213-224.*
3. De Bruyne B, Pijls NHJ, Kalesan B, et al. Fractional flow reserve-guided PCI versus medical therapy in stable coronary disease. [*N Engl J Med](https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJMoa1205361). 2012;367(11):991-1001.*
4. Xaplanteris P, Fournier S, Pijls NHJ, et al. Five-year outcomes with PCI guided by fractional flow reserve. [*N Engl J Med](https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJMoa1803538). 2018;379(3):250-259.*
5. Mehta SR, Wood DA, Storey RF, et al. Complete revascularization with multivessel PCI for myocardial infarction. [*N Engl J Med](https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJMoa1907775). 2019;381(15):1411-1421.*
6. Smits PC, Abdel-Wahab M, Neumann FJ, et al. Fractional flow reserve-guided multivessel angioplasty in myocardial infarction. [*N Engl J Med](https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJMoa1611769). 2017;376(13):1234-1244.*
7. Puymirat E, Cayla G, Simon T, et al. Multivessel PCI guided by FFR or angiography for myocardial infarction. [*N Engl J Med](https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJMoa2104650). 2021;385(4):297-308.*
8. Thiele H, Akin I, Sandri M, et al. PCI strategies in patients with acute myocardial infarction and cardiogenic shock. [*N Engl J Med](https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJMoa1710261). 2017;377(25):2419-2432.*
9. Biscaglia S, Guiducci V, Escaned J, et al. Complete or culprit-only PCI in older patients with myocardial infarction. [*N Engl J Med](https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJMoa2300468). 2023;389(10):889-898.*
10. Bohm F, Pernow J, Lindahl B, et al. FFR-guided complete or culprit-only PCI in patients with myocardial infarction. [*N Engl J Med](https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJMoa2400627). 2024;390(16):1481-1492.*
11. Politi L, Sgura F, Rossi R, et al. Timing of complete revascularization with multivessel PCI for myocardial infarction. [*N Engl J Med](https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJMoa2307823). 2023;389(15):1368-1379.*
12. Wald DS, Morris JK, Wald NJ, et al. Randomized trial of preventive angioplasty in acute myocardial infarction. [*N Engl J Med](https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJMoa1305210). 2013;369(12):1115-1123.*
13. Gershlick AH, Khan JN, Kelly DJ, et al. Randomized trial of complete versus lesion-only revascularization in patients undergoing primary percutaneous coronary intervention for STEMI and multivessel disease. [*Lancet](https://doi.org/10.1016/S0140-6736(15)60686-8). 2015;386(9991):281-290.*


### Important Reviews and Guidelines


1. 
2. Neumann FJ, Sousa-Uva M, Ahlsson A, et al. 2018 ESC/EACTS guidelines on myocardial revascularization. [*Eur Heart J](https://doi.org/10.1093/eurheartj/ehy394). 2019;40(2):87-165.*
3. Lawton JS, Tamis-Holland JE, Bangalore S, et al. 2021 ACC/AHA/SCAI guideline for coronary artery revascularization. [*Circulation](https://doi.org/10.1161/CIR.0000000000001038). 2022;145(3):e18-e114.*
4. Collet JP, Thiele H, Barbato E, et al. 2020 ESC guidelines for the management of acute coronary syndromes in patients presenting without persistent ST-segment elevation. [*Eur Heart J](https://doi.org/10.1093/eurheartj/ehaa575). 2021;42(14):1289-1367.*
5. Elgendy IY, Mahmoud AN, Kumbhani DJ, et al. Complete or culprit-only revascularization for patients with multivessel coronary artery disease undergoing percutaneous coronary intervention: a pairwise and network meta-analysis of randomized trials. [*JACC Cardiovasc Interv](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcin.2019.11.010). 2020;13(12):1413-1423.*
6. Pavasini R, Biscaglia S, Barbato E, et al. Complete revascularization reduces cardiovascular death in patients with ST-segment elevation myocardial infarction and multivessel disease. [*Eur Heart J](https://doi.org/10.1093/eurheartj/ehaa509). 2020;41(42):4103-4110.*


## Conclusions


FFR represents a major advancement in percutaneous coronary intervention, providing an objective physiological assessment standard. Key conclusions include:


1. **Stable CAD:** FFR-guided PCI superior to pure angiography-guided approach (FAME 1 & 2)
2. **STEMI with multivessel disease:** Complete revascularization superior to culprit-only (COMPLETE and others)
3. **FFR in ACS:** Safe to use for non-culprit lesion assessment
4. **Cardiogenic shock:** Culprit-only is preferred strategy (CULPRIT-SHOCK)
5. **Timing choice:** Both immediate and staged complete revascularization are acceptable
6. **Elderly patients:** Physiology-guided approach still beneficial (FIRE)


The application of FFR and iFR should be integrated with clinical judgment, imaging assessment, and patient characteristics to achieve optimal personalized treatment strategies.
